county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1831 from unorganized territory in extreme southwest Lower Michigan. County seat is St. Joseph. Named for John M. Berrien, US Attorney General. Oldest organized county in western Michigan.
county_metadata.research_tips: Berrien County Register of Deeds in St. Joseph holds land records from 1831 organization -- among the earliest in Michigan. County Clerk maintains marriage licenses from 1831 and court records. The Heritage Museum and Cultural Center in St. Joseph has genealogical resources. Berrien County was part of the early settlement frontier in southwestern Michigan. Strong Underground Railroad connections -- Berrien County was a key crossing point to Canada. The African American community in Benton Harbor and surrounding areas has deep roots. Check Berrien Springs and Niles historical materials. The Library of Michigan and Archives of Michigan hold supplementary records. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records, probate, and some church records. BLM GLO land patents from the 1830s.

source_profiles.source_name: Berrien County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1831 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.source_name: Berrien County Probate Records -- Probate Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, guardianship records, and administration files from 1831. Michigan Probate Courts also handled adoption records, mental health commitments, and name changes.

source_profiles.source_name: Berrien County Marriage Records -- County Clerk
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1831. Michigan County Clerks issue marriage licenses. State registration of marriages began 1867. Pre-1867 marriages may also be documented in church records.

source_profiles.source_name: Berrien County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Berrien County from 1840 to 1950. Michigan territorial censuses (1827, 1834) may include early residents. Michigan state censuses (1874, 1884, 1894, 1904) provide intercensal data.

source_profiles.source_name: Berrien County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan is a public land state. Original federal land patents for Berrien County are available free online at BLM GLO. Cash entry, homestead, and military bounty land warrants. Essential for identifying first landowners.

source_profiles.source_name: Underground Railroad and African American Records -- Berrien County
source_profiles.repository: Archives of Michigan / Burton Historical Collection (Detroit Public Library)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan was a free state, but the Underground Railroad was active throughout southern Michigan with routes to Canada. Berrien County records include freedom seekers' documentation, abolitionist society records, African American church records, and community histories. The Burton Historical Collection and Archives of Michigan hold related materials.

source_profiles.source_name: Berrien County Tax Rolls -- Archives of Michigan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Annual tax rolls listing property owners, assessed values, and personal property. Michigan tax rolls from 1831 at the Archives of Michigan. Essential for tracking land ownership and residency between census years.

source_profiles.source_name: Berrien County Historical Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Archives of Michigan / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers for Berrien County including local weeklies and dailies. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage and death announcements, estate settlements. Michigan Digital Newspaper Archive and Chronicling America have digitized collections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1833 from unorganized territory in south-central Lower Michigan. County seat is Coldwater. Named for John Branch, US Secretary of the Navy. Strong Quaker heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Branch County Register of Deeds in Coldwater holds land records from 1833 organization. County Clerk maintains marriage records from 1833. Probate Court has estate files. The Branch County Historical Society in Coldwater has genealogical collections. Branch County attracted Quaker settlers who were active in the Underground Railroad -- the Quaker settlement at Union City is well documented. Early migrants came from New York and Ohio. Coldwater was an important regional center. Church records (Quaker, Methodist, Baptist) are valuable for pre-civil registration research. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. The Archives of Michigan has territorial period records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1833 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1833 from unorganized territory in south-central Lower Michigan. County seat is Marshall. Named for John C. Calhoun. Marshall was a contender for state capital. Battle Creek is Cereal City.
county_metadata.research_tips: Calhoun County Register of Deeds in Marshall holds land records from 1833 organization. County Clerk maintains marriage licenses and circuit court records. Probate Court has estate files from 1833. The Calhoun County Genealogical Society has compiled indexes and family files. Marshall was nearly chosen as Michigan's state capital and has well-preserved records from the territorial era. Battle Creek is home to the Willard Library genealogy collection. Sojourner Truth resided in Battle Creek -- African American community records are valuable. Strong Underground Railroad history. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. The Adventist Heritage Center at Andrews University (Berrien Springs) has Seventh-day Adventist records relevant to Battle Creek families.

source_profiles.source_name: Calhoun County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1833 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1829 from unorganized territory in southwest Lower Michigan. County seat is Cassopolis. Named for Lewis Cass, Michigan Territorial Governor. Major Underground Railroad county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cass County Register of Deeds in Cassopolis holds land records from 1829 organization. County Clerk maintains marriage records from 1831. The Cass County Genealogy Group and local historical society have compiled family files and cemetery transcriptions. Cass County was one of Michigan's most important Underground Railroad areas -- the Quaker settlement at Penn Township and the African American community at Calvin Township (Chain Lake) are well documented. Kentucky freedmen settled in Cass County in the 1840s-1850s. The Bonine House (Underground Railroad site) records are at the Archives of Michigan. Pokagon Band of Potawatomi records are relevant for Native American research. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents from the 1830s.

source_profiles.source_name: Cass County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1829 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1869 from Emmet and Antrim in northwest Lower Michigan. County seat is Charlevoix. Named for Pierre François Xavier de Charlevoix, French explorer. Includes Beaver Island.
county_metadata.research_tips: Charlevoix County Register of Deeds in Charlevoix holds land records from 1869. County Clerk maintains marriage licenses and court records. The Charlevoix Historical Society has local genealogical materials. Beaver Island in Lake Michigan was the site of James Strang's Mormon colony (1847-1856) -- unique records related to this community. Irish fishing families later settled Beaver Island. Resort tourism developed in the late 1800s. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. Check Emmet and Antrim counties for pre-1869 records. The Archives of Michigan has Strang colony documentation. BLM GLO land patents.

source_profiles.source_name: Charlevoix County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1853 from Mackinac County at the tip of the Lower Peninsula. County seat is Cheboygan. Named from Ojibwe for 'through passage.' Gateway to the Straits of Mackinac.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cheboygan County Register of Deeds in Cheboygan holds land records from 1853 when the county was organized from Mackinac. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Cheboygan County Historical Museum preserves local records. Lumber industry was the primary economic driver. French-Canadian families from the fur trade era settled along the Cheboygan River and Straits of Mackinac. Inland Waterway (Cheboygan to Crooked Lake) records document the logging era. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. Check Mackinac County for pre-1853 records. BLM GLO land patents. The Archives of Michigan has lumber company records relevant to this area.

source_profiles.source_name: Cheboygan County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1853 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1853. Michigan County Clerks issue marriage licenses. State registration of marriages began 1867. Pre-1867 marriages may also be documented in church records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1826 from Mackinac County in the eastern Upper Peninsula. County seat is Sault Ste. Marie, one of the oldest settlements in North America. Named for the Ojibwe/Chippewa people.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chippewa County Register of Deeds in Sault Ste. Marie holds land records from 1826. One of Michigan's oldest counties. County Clerk maintains marriage records from the 1820s. Sault Ste. Marie is one of the oldest European settlements in the interior (French mission from 1668). The Chippewa County Historical Society and Bayliss Public Library have extensive local history collections. Ojibwe/Chippewa tribal records -- Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians and Bay Mills Indian Community records at tribal offices and NARA (Great Lakes Agency). Fort Brady military records at NARA. British-era fur trade records at the Archives of Michigan. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records and some church records. French-Canadian genealogy resources at Detroit Society for Genealogical Research.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1877 courthouse fire destroyed many early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1871 from Isabella County in central Lower Michigan. County seat is Harrison. Named after County Clare, Ireland. 1877 courthouse fire destroyed early records (burned county).
county_metadata.research_tips: Clare County Register of Deeds in Harrison holds land records, though the 1877 courthouse fire destroyed many early records. This is a burned county -- researchers must check the Archives of Michigan and BLM GLO for alternative sources. County Clerk has marriage records post-fire. The Clare County Historical Society has reconstructed some genealogical data from surviving sources. Clare County was lumber country; company records may fill gaps. For pre-1877 records, check Isabella County (parent). Tax rolls at the Archives of Michigan may have survived. FamilySearch microfilm covers post-fire vital records. Federal census records (1870, 1880) provide pre-fire population data.

source_profiles.source_name: Clare County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1877 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens. Some gaps due to 1877 courthouse fire destroyed many early records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1839 from unorganized territory in central Lower Michigan. County seat is St. Johns. Named for DeWitt Clinton, New York governor. Strong German-Catholic heritage at Westphalia.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clinton County Register of Deeds in St. Johns holds land records from 1839 organization. County Clerk maintains marriage licenses from 1839 and circuit court records. Probate Court has estate files from 1839. The Clinton County Historical Society has genealogical materials. German immigrants settled heavily in Clinton County; check German Lutheran and Catholic church records (St. Peter's, Westphalia is an important German Catholic parish with records from the 1830s). Westphalia and surrounding communities have strong German-Catholic heritage. Early settlers also came from New York and New England. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records and some church records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan supplementary.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1879 from unorganized territory in north-central Lower Michigan. County seat is Grayling. Named for Col. William Crawford. Heart of Michigan's white pine lumber country.
county_metadata.research_tips: Crawford County Register of Deeds in Grayling holds land records from 1879 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. Crawford County was the heart of Michigan's white pine lumber country -- the AuSable River was a major log-driving waterway. Lumber camp records and company towns are documented at the Archives of Michigan. The Hartwick Pines Logging Museum near Grayling preserves lumbering heritage. Crawford County was sparsely settled after the lumber era ended. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Check military records for Camp Grayling (Michigan National Guard training site established 1913).

source_profiles.source_name: Crawford County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
